It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.

Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.

end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 45 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 1.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the UP transitions, I can see a clear pattern:
When the right square is at cols 39-43:
- Transition 6: left (44,48) -> (39,43), right (44,48) -> (44,48) [blocked]
- Transition 16: left (44,48) -> (39,43), right (49,53) -> (44,48)
- Transition 29: left (44,48) -> (39,43), right (44,48) -> (44,48) [blocked]
When the right square is at cols 34-38:
- Transition 40: left (39,43), right (44,48) -> no change
- Transition 42: left (44,48) -> (39,43), right (49,53) -> (44,48)
So when the right square is at cols 34-38:
- If left is at (39,43) and right is at (44,48), UP doesn't work (transition 40).
- If left is at (44,48) and right is at (49,53), UP works (transition 42).
This suggests that when the right square is at cols 34-38, it can only move UP from rows 49-53 to rows 44-48, not from rows 44-48 to rows 39-43.
So the right square's vertical range depends on its column position:
- At cols 34-38: rows 44-53 (2 positions: 44-48 and 49-53)
- At cols 39-43: rows 44-58 (3 positions: 44-48, 49-53, and 54-58)
Wait, but looking at transition 13:
- Before: left (49,53), right (54,58)
- After: left (44,48), right (49,53)
So when the right square was at rows 54-58, cols 39-43, it moved UP to rows 49-53.
And looking at transition 27:
- Before: left (54,58), right (54,58)
- After: left (49,53), right (49,53)
So when both squares were at rows 54-58, they moved UP to rows 49-53.
This is getting complicated. Let me think about the left square's vertical range.
Looking at the transitions, the left square's vertical range seems to be:
- rows 39-43 (top)
- rows 44-48
- rows 49-53
- rows 54-58 (bottom)
And the right square's vertical range depends on its column position:
- At cols 34-38: rows 44-53 (2 positions: 44-48 and 49-53)
- At cols 39-43: rows 44-58 (3 positions: 44-48, 49-53, and 54-58)
So the left square has a larger vertical range than the right square when the right square is at cols 34-38.
Now, the key observation is that when the left square is at rows 39-43 and the right square is at rows 44-48 (with right at cols 34-38), pressing UP doesn't work (transition 40). This is because the left square can't move UP (at top of range), and the right square can't move UP (at top of its range at cols 34-38).
So the squares move together vertically, and both need to have space to move. If one is blocked, neither moves.
Wait, but transition 6 shows that when the right square was blocked, the left square still moved. Let me re-check.
Transition 6: Before: left (44,48), right (44,48); After: left (39,43), right (44,48)
So the left square moved UP from rows 44-48 to rows 39-43, and the right square stayed at rows 44-48. This means the squares can move independently vertically.
But transition 40 shows that when the left square is at rows 39-43 and the right square is at rows 44-48 (with right at cols 34-38), pressing UP doesn't work. This is because the left square can't move UP (at top of range), and the right square can't move UP (at top of its range at cols 34-38).
So the squares can move independently vertically, but each square can only move if it has space to move in that direction.
OK so now I understand the mechanics better. The squares move independently, each constrained by their own vertical and horizontal ranges.
Now, what's the goal? I've been exploring the movement mechanics, but I haven't found the goal yet. Let me think about what the goal might be.
Looking at the maze structure:
- The left square is in a yellow chamber.
- The right square is in an orange chamber.
- There's a narrow passage between the chambers at cols 29-33.
Maybe the goal is to bring the squares to specific positions that trigger a level completion. Or maybe the goal is to use MOUSE to click on something.
Actually, let me try a completely different approach. Let me look at the initial state and see if there's a target position for each square. The initial state was:
- Left square: rows 49-53, cols 19-23
- Right square: rows 49-53, cols 39-43
And the current state is:
- Left square: rows 39-43, cols 19-23
- Right square: rows 44-48, cols 34-38
Maybe the goal is to bring both squares to rows 39-43. But the right square can't reach rows 39-43 when it's at cols 34-38. So I need to move the right square to cols 39-43 first.
But pressing RIGHT didn't work because the right square was blocked from moving RIGHT (wall at cols 39-43). So I need to move the right square to a position where it can move RIGHT.
Looking at the ascii, the right square is at rows 44-48, cols 34-38. To the right of the right square (cols 39-43) is a wall (B). So the right square can't move RIGHT from this position.
But when the right square is at rows 49-53, cols 34-38, the cells to the right (cols 39-43) are also walls (B). So the right square can't move RIGHT from rows 49-53 either.
Wait, but looking at the ascii for rows 49-53:
'BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBYYYOOBBBBBBBBBB'
- cols 34-43: B (wall)
So the right square can't move RIGHT when it's at rows 49-53, cols 34-38.
But earlier, when the right square was at rows 49-53, cols 39-43, pressing RIGHT moved it to cols 44-48. So the right square can move RIGHT when it's at cols 39-43.
So the right square's horizontal range depends on its row position:
- At rows 44-48: cols 34-38 (can't move RIGHT, wall at cols 39-43)
- At rows 49-53: cols 34-48 (can move RIGHT from cols 34-38 to cols 39-43, and from cols 39-43 to cols 44-48)
Wait, but looking at the ascii for rows 49-53:
'BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBYYYOOBBBBBBBBBB'
- cols 34-43: B (wall)
So there's a wall at cols 34-43. This means the right square can't be at cols 34-38 when it's at rows 49-53.
But transition 42 shows:
- Before: left (44,48), right (49,53)
- After: left (39,43), right (44,48)
So the right square was at rows 49-53, cols 34-38 before the transition. This contradicts the ascii.
Oh wait, the ascii I'm looking at is the current frame, not the frame before transition 42. The ascii might have changed between transitions.
Actually, no. The ascii is the current frame, and the right square is at rows 44-48, cols 34-38. The ascii shows the maze structure, which includes the squares. So the cells at rows 44-48, cols 34-38 are S (sky blue), not B (black).
Let me re-examine the ascii:
Rows 44-48: 'BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBYYYOOSSSSSBBBBB'
- cols 14-28: B (wall)
- cols 29-31: Y (yellow passage)
- cols 32-33: O (orange passage)
- cols 34-38: S (right square)
- cols 39-43: B (wall)
So the right square is at cols 34-38, and there's a wall at cols 39-43. This means the right square can't move RIGHT from this position.
But when the right square is at rows 49-53, the ascii shows:
Rows 49-53: 'BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBYYYOOBBBBBBBBBB'
- cols 34-43: B (wall)
So there's a wall at cols 34-43 when the right square is at rows 49-53. But the right square was at cols 34-38 when it was at rows 49-53 (transition 42). This means the right square is overlapping with the wall, which doesn't make sense.
Oh wait, I think the issue is that the ascii includes the squares as part of the maze. So when the right square is at rows 49-53, cols 34-38, the cells at rows 49-53, cols 34-38 are S (sky blue), not B (black). The ascii I'm looking at is the current frame, where the right square is at rows 44-48, cols 34-38. So the cells at rows 49-53, cols 34-38 are B (black) because the right square is not there.
OK so the maze structure is:
- When the right square is at rows 44-48, cols 34-38, the cells at rows 44-48, cols 34-38 are S (sky blue).
- When the right square is at rows 49-53, cols 34-38, the cells at rows 49-53, cols 34-38 are S (sky blue).
- The cells at rows 49-53, cols 39-43 are B (wall), so the right square can't move RIGHT from rows 49-53, cols 34-38.
But wait, earlier I saw that when the right square was at rows 49-53, cols 39-43, pressing RIGHT moved it to cols 44-48. So the right square can be at cols 39-43 when it's at rows 49-53.
This is confusing. Let me just try to figure out the goal by exploring the state space more systematically.
Actually, I realize I've been spending too many actions exploring the mechanics. Let me try a different approach: maybe the goal is to bring both squares to the same row and column position, or to specific target positions.
Looking at the maze structure, there's a narrow passage at cols 29-33 that connects the yellow and orange chambers. Maybe the goal is to bring both squares to this passage.
But the left square's horizontal range is cols 14-23, and the right square's horizontal range is cols 34-48. Neither square can reach cols 29-33.
Actually, wait. Let me check if the left square can move RIGHT when the right square can also move RIGHT. Looking at transition 13:
- RIGHT: left (44,48,19,23) -> (44,48,24,28), right (49,53,39,43) -> (49,53,44,48)
So the left square moved from cols 19-23 to cols 24-28, and the right square moved from cols 39-43 to cols 44-48.
So the left square's horizontal range includes cols 24-28. This means the left square can reach cols 24-28, which is closer to the passage at cols 29-33.
But the left square's horizontal range is cols 14-28 (2 positions: 14-18 and 19-23, and possibly 24-28). And the right square's horizontal range is cols 34-48 (3 positions: 34-38, 39-43, and 44-48).
So the squares can't reach the passage at cols 29-33. The goal must be something else.
Let me try a completely different approach. Maybe the goal is to use MOUSE to click on the squares or on specific cells. Let me try clicking on the left square.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try clicking on the left square with MOUSE
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 41, 'col': 21}])
print("Result:", result)

# Check square positions after MOUSE
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Sky blue: row=[{min(rows)},{max(rows)}], col=[{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
